session_metadata.title: The Two Ordinances: Baptism and Communion
session_metadata.session_topic: Doctrinal exposition of the two church ordinances: Believers Baptism and the Lord's Supper, their biblical basis, mode, recipients, meaning, and local-church practice.

theological_themes: identification with Christ's death and resurrection
the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
first day of the week worship
John 3:5, born of water and Spirit

chunks.content: The two ordinances are Believers Baptism and the Lord's Supper. Ordinances are practices ordered and commanded by the Lord for his people to observe; these two are considered the primary ordinances of the church.
themes: the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
claims: There are two primary church ordinances: Believers Baptism and the Lord's Supper.
An ordinance is something ordered and commanded by the Lord for his people to practice.

chunks.content: Baptism was commanded by the Lord Jesus (Matt. 28:18-20) and practiced by the early church (Acts 8:36-38). Baptism is taken by a person who has repented and believed on the Lord Jesus Christ's death and resurrection (Acts 2:38-41). Baptism is given by immersion (Acts 8:38) voluntarily in the name of the Father, Son and Holy Spirit (cf. Matt. 28:19). Baptism is a public confession, an outward expression or symbol of what a person believes internally, identifying with Christ's death through immersion (dead to the old self) and with Christ's resurrection (giving new life) (Romans 6:1-4; Gal 3:25-27).
themes: identification with Christ's death and resurrection
claims: Baptism is commanded by Christ in the Great Commission.
Baptism is for those who have already repented and believed.
Baptism is administered by immersion.
Baptism is administered voluntarily in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
Baptism is a public, outward symbol of an internal reality of union with Christ in his death and resurrection.

chunks.content: Cited summary (attributed in source to Piper): 'We believe that baptism is an ordinance of the Lord by which those who have repented and come to faith express their union with Christ in his death and resurrection, by being immersed in water in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit. It is a sign of belonging to the new people of God, the true Israel, and an emblem of burial and cleansing, signifying death to the old life of unbelief, and purification from the pollution of sin.'
themes: identification with Christ's death and resurrection
claims: Baptism expresses the union of repentant believers with Christ in his death and resurrection.
Baptism signifies belonging to the new people of God (the true Israel).
Baptism is an emblem of burial and of cleansing from the pollution of sin.

chunks.content: Why baptism? Because it is officially ordained by Jesus. Christ commanded it in Matthew 28:19-20: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you.' The early church gave it an important place (Acts 2:38, 41; 8:12-13, 36, 38; 9:18; 10:47-48; 16:15, 33; 18:8; 19:5). Baptism publicly declares that one is now a follower of Jesus Christ. When those in Acts were baptized they were communicating to all around them that they had decided to follow Jesus. It is the outward sign of the inward reality that we are now in Christ, identified with his death (the death of our old self) and his resurrection which gives us new life (Romans 6:3-5; Galatians 3:27; Colossians 2:9-13). It is one of the foundational truths (Heb 6:1-2 alluded to as '6:1-2').
themes: identification with Christ's death and resurrection
claims: Baptism is mandated by Christ's Great Commission.
The early church practiced baptism extensively as recorded throughout Acts.
Baptism is a public declaration that one has become a follower of Jesus.
Baptism is the outward sign of an inward spiritual reality.
Baptism is among the foundational truths of the Christian faith.

chunks.content: Romans 6:3-4: 'Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in the newness of life.' Faith is the means by which we are united to Christ and justified, but we show, say, signify, and symbolize that faith with the act of baptism. Faith unites to Christ; baptism symbolizes the union. Analogy: 'With this ring I thee wed'. The ring does not make the marriage; the covenant-making vows do. The ring shows and symbolizes the covenant. So with faith and baptism. Baptism dramatically portrays what happened spiritually when one received Christ: the old self of unbelief, rebellion, and idolatry died, and a new self of faith, submission, and treasuring Christ came into being. That is what is confessed to the world and to heaven in baptism.
themes: identification with Christ's death and resurrection
claims: Faith, not baptism, is the instrumental means of union with Christ and justification.
Baptism functions as the public sign and symbol of the union faith creates.
Baptism dramatically portrays the spiritual reality of dying to the old self and rising to new life in Christ.

chunks.content: Baptism by immersion. Romans 6:3-4: being buried under water and coming up out of the water signifies rising from the grave. The Greek word 'baptism' means dip or immerse, and most scholars agree this is how the early church practiced baptism; sprinkling and pouring emerge only much later in the historical evidence. Acts 8:37-39: Philip and the eunuch 'went down into the water' and 'came up out of the water,' indicating immersion. John 3:23: John was baptizing at Aenon near Salim 'because water was plentiful there'. Plentiful water is unnecessary if one is merely sprinkling; only a jar would be needed. Note: in the household baptisms recorded in the New Testament, the age of the child is not mentioned, so they cannot be straightforwardly used to establish infant baptism.
claims: The Greek meaning of 'baptizo' (dip/immerse) supports immersion as the proper mode.
The early church practiced baptism by immersion; sprinkling/pouring is a later development.
Acts 8:38-39 describes going down into and coming up out of the water, consistent with immersion.
John 3:23 implies plentiful water was needed because immersion was practiced.
Household-baptism passages do not specify the ages of those baptized and therefore do not establish infant baptism.

chunks.content: Baptism is to be administered in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19). There is a holy appeal to God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Spirit to be present in this act. There is no salvation without the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. When we call on their name, we depend upon them, honor them, and declare that this act is because of them, by them, and for them.
claims: Baptism must be performed in the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit.
The Trinitarian formula is an appeal to and dependence on each Person of the Godhead.
Salvation involves all three Persons of the Trinity.

chunks.content: Baptism is an expression of faith, only for believers. The order is always: believe and then be baptized (Matt. 3:2-6; 28:19; Acts 2:37-38; 16:14-15, 34). Matthew 28:19: 'make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.' Acts 2:38: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.' Acts 16:14-15: Lydia heard Paul, the Lord opened her heart to pay attention, and after she was baptized she urged them to come to her house. See also Acts 19:1-6. Galatians 3:25-27: 'now that faith has come, we are no longer under a guardian, for in Christ Jesus you are all sons of God, through faith. For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.'
claims: Baptism is only for those who have come to faith.
The scriptural order is always belief first, then baptism.
Discipling and baptizing are linked in the Great Commission.
Baptism is preceded by repentance and the gift of the Holy Spirit follows.
Through faith we are sons of God in Christ; baptism into Christ corresponds to having put on Christ.

chunks.content: Baptism in the local church. Two parties are involved, and a person does not baptize themselves (Acts 8, the Ethiopian eunuch). The church has been given the mandate to baptize. Acts 2:41: 'So those who received his word were baptized, and there were added that day about three thousand souls.' The Great Commission was given to the apostles and by extension implied to the church; the church recognises the believer and his profession of faith in Jesus, and is in some ways responsible for the believer's growth in the Lord. So it makes all the more sense for a person to be baptised under the church. Baptism is where faith goes public (Acts 2:38-41, those who listened agreed to believe and take baptism, expressing publicly what they believed). It is also tied to being added to the body of Christ, taking part of the communion, and involving oneself in the worship time (the remembrance meeting).
claims: A person cannot baptize themselves; baptism requires another party.
The mandate to baptize has been given to the local church.
The local church recognises a believer's profession of faith.
Baptism is the moment where personal faith is made public.
Baptism is connected to being added to the body of Christ and to participation in communion and the remembrance meeting.

chunks.content: Process for baptism in the local church: (a) Once a person becomes a true born-again believer they should seek baptism (Acts 8:12; Acts 16:33; Matthew 28:19). (b) The candidate will meet with the elders, discuss their faith, and attend some classes on baptism. (c) Baptism will be done as soon as possible. (d) If the candidate is not from a Christian background, a signed affidavit will be wanted declaring that they are taking baptism out of their own free will. (e) Believers will be encouraged to be publicly baptized. They should be willing not to remain secret believers, handled case by case.
claims: Baptism candidates must be true born-again believers.
Candidates must meet with elders for an interview and instruction.
Baptism should be administered as soon as possible after readiness is confirmed.
A signed affidavit of free-will consent is required from candidates from non-Christian backgrounds.
Public baptism is the encouraged norm; secrecy is to be discouraged though handled case-by-case.

chunks.content: John 3:5: 'Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God.' Some look at this passage and argue that a person must be baptized first to be born again. But other Acts passages show baptism follows repentance and belief in Jesus, so baptismal-regeneration as the meaning of John 3:5 does not fit the wider scriptural data. Three interpretations of 'water' in this verse are surveyed: (a) water baptism, already ruled out as misaligned with the rest of Scripture and not the focus of this passage; (b) water of physical birth, since Nicodemus asks how one can be born again, some take 'water' as amniotic waters of natural birth, but being born again is not physical (the physical nature is already corrupted by sin) but spiritual; (c) water as cleansing, since Nicodemus, a Pharisee versed in the Old Testament, would likely have understood Jesus to be alluding to Ezekiel 36:25-27 and Isaiah 44:3, where God promises cleansing by water from idols and the giving of his Spirit producing a new heart enabling walking with the Lord. On this reading Jesus is teaching that the cleansing and Spirit-given new heart spoken of in Ezekiel are needed for one to be born again. Resources cited: Josh Moody, John 1-12 for You; The Gospel Coalition article 'Born of Water and Spirit'.
themes: John 3:5, born of water and Spirit
claims: John 3:5 should not be read as teaching baptismal regeneration, since other Acts passages show baptism follows belief.
There are three main interpretations of 'water' in John 3:5.
The physical-birth (amniotic) interpretation conflicts with the spiritual nature of regeneration.
The most contextually fitting interpretation is that 'water' refers to Old Testament cleansing imagery (Ezekiel 36; Isaiah 44) which Nicodemus as a Pharisee would have recognised.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: 'Water' in John 3:5 refers to water baptism. Baptism is required for regeneration.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Surface reading of the verse pairing 'water' and 'Spirit' as parallel preconditions for entering the kingdom. Rejected here because Acts shows baptism follows repentance and belief, and baptism is not the focus of John 3.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: 'Water' refers to amniotic waters of physical birth, natural birth contrasted with spiritual rebirth.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Nicodemus had just asked how one can be born when old; reading 'water' as natural birth provides a contrast with Spirit-birth. Rejected here because being born again is not physical (physical nature is already corrupted) but spiritual.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: 'Water' refers to Old Testament cleansing imagery: God washing his people from idols and giving them a new heart by the Spirit.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Nicodemus, a Pharisee versed in the OT, would have recognised an allusion to Ezekiel 36:25-27 and Isaiah 44:3, where cleansing by water and the giving of the Spirit produce a new heart, the very prerequisites of new birth. Preferred here as best fitting context.

chunks.content: Recap on ordinances. An ordinance is something which has been ordered and commanded by the Lord for his people to practice. Believers Baptism and the Lord's Supper are the primary ordinances. Some may also consider washing of feet, marriage broadly, and praying for the sick from James 5; but the primary ordinances considered here are baptism and the Lord's Supper. Ordinances have the idea of a symbol and in themselves do not have any (saving) power.
claims: Ordinances are commands of the Lord to be practiced by his people.
Ordinances are symbolic and do not in themselves possess saving power.
Some Christians extend the category of ordinance to include foot-washing, marriage, and anointing of the sick (James 5), but the primary two recognised here are baptism and the Lord's Supper.

chunks.content: The Lord's Supper (the Breaking of Bread) was instituted by Jesus Christ (Matt. 26:26-29) and followed by the apostles (1 Cor. 11:23-32). It signifies the remembrance of Christ, the proclamation of his death, and the assurance of Christ's second coming (1 Cor. 11:24, 26; Matt. 26:29). It is a meeting to worship the Lord Jesus Christ and to remember the new covenant he instituted through his death. Why? Jesus instituted it (Matt. 26:26-29); the apostles followed it (1 Cor. 11:23-32); and it brings our attention to Christ and his work on the cross every week.
themes: the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
claims: Jesus Christ personally instituted the Lord's Supper.
The apostles continued to observe and teach it.
The Lord's Supper signifies remembrance of Christ, proclamation of his death, and assurance of his second coming.
The Lord's Supper centers worship on the new covenant Christ instituted through his death.
It brings our attention weekly to Christ and his work on the cross.

chunks.content: Requirements for partaking. (a) Those who have believed in Jesus: all the disciples partook and they believed in Jesus; Acts 2:42: 'They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer'; Acts 20:7: 'upon the first day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them.' (b) Those who have been baptised: there is no explicit verse stating that only baptised believers may take part of the Lord's table, but it is observed that (1) the regeneration experience and baptism went hand in hand (Acts 2:41-42); baptism shows a person becomes one with the body of Christ, internal in regeneration and external at baptism; (2) baptism signifies our identification with Christ in his death, the death of our old man, and his resurrection giving new life (Romans 6:3-5; Galatians 3:27; Colossians 2:9-13); (3) since baptism is integral to a person's salvation experience and being added to the body, and since communion is for those who believe in Jesus, it is ideal for those who are born again and baptised to take part of the Lord's table.
themes: the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
first day of the week worship
claims: Only those who have believed in Jesus should take the Lord's Supper.
Scripture does not give an explicit command that only the baptised may partake, but baptism and regeneration are paired in Acts.
Baptism externalises union with the body of Christ that began internally at regeneration.
It is therefore ideal that those partaking be both born again and baptised.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Strict view: only baptised believers should partake of the Lord's Supper.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: Although no explicit verse demands it, the consistent pairing of regeneration and baptism in Acts 2:41-42 and the meaning of baptism as identification with Christ's body (Rom 6:3-5; Gal 3:27; Col 2:9-13) make it ideal that only the baptised partake.

perspectives_within_chunk.view: Acknowledged tension: there is no explicit New Testament statement restricting the Table to baptised believers only.
perspectives_within_chunk.reasoning: The text concedes 'There isn't an explicit statement that says only baptised believers can take part of the Lord's table', the requirement is inferred rather than commanded.

chunks.content: Self-examination. Every believer should examine themselves before partaking of the emblems, being aware that it is an act of worship and should be taken in a worthy manner (1 Corinthians 11:27-29). If there is unforgiveness, an issue with a fellow believer, or any sin issue, that should be resolved before partaking (Matthew 5:23-24). Unrepentant believers under church discipline were excluded from the Supper (1 Cor. 5:11-13; 2 Thess. 3:6, 11-15).
themes: the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
claims: Believers must examine themselves before partaking of communion.
Communion is an act of worship and must be taken in a worthy manner.
Unresolved sin or relational rupture should be addressed before partaking.
Unrepentant believers under church discipline were excluded from the Lord's Supper in the New Testament.

chunks.content: Frequency. The Lord's Supper is to be observed on the first day of the week. Acts 20:7: 'And upon the first day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them, ready to depart on the morrow; and continued his speech until midnight.' The Didache and Justin Martyr's First Apology indicate that weekly administration of the Lord's Supper existed in the early church (cited via The Gospel Coalition article 'The Frequency of Communion Calmly Considered').
themes: the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
first day of the week worship
claims: The biblical pattern is to observe the Lord's Supper on the first day of the week.
Acts 20:7 supports a weekly gathering for the breaking of bread.
Early Christian sources (Didache; Justin Martyr's First Apology) attest to weekly observance.

chunks.content: Process of the meeting. During the meeting, the men, when led by the Spirit, can spontaneously share a passage of scripture, prayer, or song in line with the purpose of this meeting (1 Corinthians 14:26). The time will conclude with the communion and then an intentional time to offer unto the Lord financially and in other ways of giving of one's life to the Lord.
themes: the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
first day of the week worship
claims: Men, when led by the Spirit, may spontaneously share scripture, prayer, or song during the meeting.
The shared contributions must align with the purpose of the breaking-of-bread meeting.
The meeting concludes with communion and an intentional time of offering to the Lord (financially and in life-giving ways).

chunks.content: Visitors and the Table. Those visiting are welcome to participate from the table if they are following the same practice in another Bible-believing church under the conviction of the elders.
themes: the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
claims: Visiting believers are welcome at the Lord's Table.
The condition is that they are walking in the same practice in another Bible-believing church.
Participation is under the conviction and oversight of the elders.

chunks.content: Resources cited in the document: (a) Desiring God, 'What Is Baptism and How Important Is It' (desiringgod.org). (b) Bobby Jamieson, 'Understanding Baptism'. (c) Charles Caldwell Ryrie, 'Basic Theology: A Popular Systematic Guide to Understanding Biblical Truth' (Moody Publishers, Kindle Edition, p. 488). (d) The Gospel Coalition, 'Born of Water and Spirit' article. (e) The Gospel Coalition, 'The Frequency of Communion Calmly Considered'. (f) Josh Moody, 'John 1-12 for You'.
themes: the Lord's Supper / breaking of bread
John 3:5, born of water and Spirit
claims: External resources are cited to support the document's positions on baptism, communion, and John 3:5.

raw_extracted_text:
The two ordinances -- Baptism and Communion

Ordinances

Ordinances are ordered and commanded by the Lord. Believers Baptism and The Lord's supper being the primary ones.

Believers Baptism

We believe that Baptism was commanded by Lord Jesus (Matt. 28:18-20) and practiced by the early church (Acts 8:36-38). Baptism is taken by a person who have repented and believed on the Lord Jesus Christ's death and resurrection (Acts 2:38-41). Baptism is given by immersion (Acts 8:38) voluntarily in the name of the Father, Son and Holy Spirit (Matt. 2:19). Baptism is a public confession of an outward expression (symbol) of what a person believes internally identifying with Christ's death through immersion, dead to the old self and with Christ's resurrection giving new life. (Romans 6:1-4; Gal 3:25 -- 27)

The Lord's Supper

The Lord's supper was instituted by Jesus Christ (Matt. 26:26-29) and followed by the apostles (1 Cor. 11:23-32). It signifies the remembrance of Christ, proclamation of His death and assurance of Christ's second coming (1 Cor. 11:24,26). The Lord's supper was conducted on the 1st day of the week, and it is to be taken by those who have believed Jesus and taken baptism (Since baptism is an integral part after the salvation of a person) (Acts 2:42, 20:7, Romans 6:3-5; Galatians 3:27; Colossians 2:9-13).

Every believer should examine themselves before partaking in the Lord's Supper. (1 Corinthians 11:27-29). During the meeting the men, when led by the Spirit, can spontaneously share a passage of scripture, prayer, or song in line with the purpose of this meeting (1 Corinthians 14:26). The time will conclude with the communion and then an intentional time to offer unto the Lord financially and other ways of giving of your life to the Lord.

Those visiting are welcome to participate from the table if they are following the same in another Bible believing church under the conviction of the elders.

Ordinances of the church

Ordinances has the idea of a symbol and in itself does have any power. Ordinance is something this is order by Christ himself which we consider primarily as baptism and The's Lords supper. Some may even consider washing of feet broadly marriage and praying for the sick form James chapter 5. But we wound consider the baptism and The's Lord's supper.

Ordinances 1 - Water Baptism

We believe that baptism is an ordinance of the Lord by which those who have repented and come to faith express their union with Christ in his death and resurrection, by being immersed in water in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit. It is a sign of belonging to the new people of God, the true Israel, and an emblem of burial and cleansing, signifying death to the old life of unbelief, and purification from the pollution of sin. -- Piper

1) Why Baptism? Ordained (Officially ordered) by Jesus.
  a) Commanded by Christ
    i) Matthew 28:19-20: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you.'
  b) The early church gave an important place to baptism (Acts 2:38, 41; 8:12-13, 36, 38; 9:18; 10:47-48; 16:15,33; 18:8; 19:5).
  c) To publicly declare that you are now a follower of Jesus Christ. When those in Acts were baptized they were communicating that they had made the decision to follow Jesus to all who were around them. - Acts 2:38, 41; 8:12-13.
  d) Baptism is the outward sign of the inward reality.
    i) Baptism is the outward sign of the inward reality that we now are in Christ and we are identified with Christ in His death, the death of our old man, and His resurrection which gives us new life Romans 6:3-5; Galatians 3:27; Colossians 2:9-13.
    ii) Romans 6:3-4 - Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in the newness of life.
    iii) Faith is the means by which we are united to Christ and justified. But we show this faith --- we say this faith and signify this faith and symbolize this faith --- with the act of baptism. Faith unites to Christ; baptism symbolizes the union.
      (1) Ex: An analogy would be saying, 'With this ring I thee wed.' When we say that, we don't mean that the ring or the putting of the ring on the finger is what makes us married. No, it shows the covenant and symbolizes the covenant, but the covenant-making vows make the marriage. So it is with faith and baptism.
    iv) Baptism dramatically portrays what happened spiritually when you received Christ: Your old self of unbelief and rebellion and idolatry died, and a new you of faith and submission and treasuring Christ came into being. That's what you confess to the world and to heaven when you are baptized.
  e) It is one of the foundational truths -- (6:1-2)

2) Baptism by immersion
  a) Romans 6:3-4
    i) You are buried under water and then come out of from the water to signify rising from the grave.
    ii) The word baptism in Greek means dip or immerse. And most scholars agree that this is the way the early church practiced baptism. Only much later does the practice of sprinkling or pouring emerge, as far as we can tell from the evidence.
  b) Acts 8:37-39 - And he commanded the chariot to stop, and they both went down into the water, Philip and the eunuch, and he baptized him. And when they came up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord carried Philip away, and the eunuch saw him no more, and went on his way rejoicing.
  c) John 3:23 - 23 John also was baptizing at Aenon near Salim, because water was plentiful there, and people were coming and being baptized
    i) You don't need plentiful water if you are simply sprinkling. You just need a jar.
  d) Child baptism -- the age of the child is not mentioned in any of the household baptisms.

3) In the name of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit.
  a) Matthew 28:19: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.'
  b) There is a holy appeal to God the Father and God the Son and God the Holy Spirit to be present in this act. There is no salvation without the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. When we call on their name, we depend upon them and honor them and say that this act is because of them and by them and for them.

4) Expression of faith
  a) Only for believers. Believe and be baptised.
  b) That the scriptural order is always believe and then be baptized (Matt. 3:2-6; 28:19; Acts 2:37-38; 16:14-15, 34);
    i) Mat 28:19 Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,
    ii) Act 2:38 And Peter said to them, 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.
    iii) Act 16:14-15 - One who heard us was a woman named Lydia, from the city of Thyatira, a seller of purple goods, who was a worshiper of God. The Lord opened her heart to pay attention to what was said by Paul. And after she was baptized, and her household as well, she urged us, saying, 'If you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come to my house and stay.' And she prevailed upon us
    iv) Acts. 19:1-6
    i) Gal 3:25 - 27 - But now that faith has come, we are no longer under a guardian, for in Christ Jesus you are all sons of God, through faith. For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.

Covered till here

5) Baptism in the Local church
  a) Two parties involved
    i) Someone does not do it to themself - Acts 8 -- The Eunuch
    ii) The church has been given the mandate to baptize.
    iii) Act 2:41 So those who received his word were baptized, and there were added that day about three thousand souls.
    iv) The great commissions were given to the apostles and later implied to the church. The church in some sense recognises the believer and his profession of faith in Jesus and then they are in some ways responsible in the growing of that believer in the Lord. So, it makes all the more sense for the person to take baptism under the church.
  b) Where faith goes public
    i) Acts 2:38-41 - The people who listened to the message came out from the crowd and agreed to believe and take baptism. That was a way for them to express that to the people out of what they believed.
  c) Adding to the body of Christ
  d) Taking part of the communion
  e) Involving yourself in the worship time (remembrance meeting)

6) Process
  a) Once a person becomes a true born-again believer, they should seek to get baptised Acts 8:12; Acts 16:33; Matthew 28:19
  b) Will have to meet the elders and discuss their faith and some classes on baptism.
  c) Will be done as soon as possible
  d) Will want signed affidavit if not a Christian declaring that they are taking baptism out of their own free will.
  e) Will encourage believers to be publicly baptized (should be willing to not be secret believers) - Case by case

John 3:5
1. Joh 3:5 Jesus answered, 'Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God.'
2. Some look at this passage and say a person has to be baptised 1st to be born again. Is this true or not.
  a. 1st we know when we look at other passages from Acts that someone is baptised after repenting and believing in Jesus. Baptism is for some who is already a believer and so this interpretation doesn't add up. How
3. There are 3 interpretations to this passage
  a. Water baptism - Water meaning baptism which we already have discussed that it does add up from other passages. Also, we don't see anything about baptism mentioned in this passage overall. In fact baptism is not the focus on this passage.
  b. Water of birth -- Since the context here is about being born again and Nicodemus asks how can one be born again, some say it is talking about water of birth, where the mothers water breaks in order for a person to be born. But we know that to be born again in the Lord is not physical since already corrupted by sinful nature but more spiritual.
  c. Water as in cleaning of one person -- Nicodemus is a Pharisees and knows the O.T. scriptures and when he ask how can one person be born again Jesus gives this explanation. Seems like Nicodemus would have understood it and that may be because of Ezekiel 36:25-27 and Isaiah 44:3 which talks about water and baptism. In the Ezekiel context God is saying there will be revival by cleaning through water among them from their idols and Spirit will be given to them which will give them a new heart and allow them to walk in the Lord. So he is telling this cleansing and giving of a new heart through the Spirit is needed for a person to be born again.
  d. Resources
    i. Josh Moody -- John 1-12 for you
    ii. https://www.thegospelcoalition.org/article/born-water-spirit-mean/

Ordinances

Ordinances is something which has been ordered and commanded by the Lord for his people to practise. Believers Baptism and The Lord's supper being the primary ones.

Believers Baptism

[Repeat of opening summary on Believers Baptism, see above.]

Ordinance 2 -- The Lord's Supper

The Breaking of Bread, or Lord's Supper, meeting is a time to worship the Lord Jesus Christ and remember the new covenant He instituted through His death.

1) Why?
  a) Jesus instituted it.
    i) Matt. 26:26-29
  b) Apostles followed it
    i) 1 Cor. 11:23-32
  c) It brings our attention to Christ and His work on the cross every week.

2) What does it signify?
  a) It is a remembrance of Christ (1 Cor. 11:24).
  b) Proclamation of His death (1 Cor. 11:26).
  c) Assurance of Christ's second coming (Matt. 26:29; 1 Cor. 11:26).

3) Requirements
  a) Those who believed in Jesus.
    i) We see that all the disciples did take part of the Lord's supper and they believed in Jesus
    ii) Acts 2:42 They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer. Everyone was filled with awe at the many wonders and signs performed by the apostles. All the believers were together and had everything in common.
    iii) Act 20:7 And upon the first day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them, ready to depart on the morrow; and continued his speech until midnight.
  b) Those who have been baptised.
    i) There isn't an explicit statement that says only baptised believers can take part of the Lord's table.
    ii) But we observe in the following verse that
      (1) Regeneration experience and baptism went hand in hand. Acts 2:41-42. Baptism also shows that one person becomes one with body of Christ. Even though this is internal with respect to regeneration but it becomes a external reality at baptism.
      (2) Baptism also signifies we are identified with Christ in His death, the death of our old man, and His resurrection which gives us new life Romans 6:3-5; Galatians 3:27; Colossians 2:9-13.
      (3) Hence since baptism is a very integral part of the salvation experience of a person and being added to the body and since the communion is for those who believe in Jesus, it is ideal for those who are born again, baptised to take part of the Lord's table.
  c) Examining: Every believer should examine themselves before partaking of the emblems, being aware that it is an act of worship and should be taken in a worthy manner (1 Corinthians 11:27-29). If there is unforgiveness or an issue with a fellow believer or any sin issue, that should be resolved before partaking of the emblems Matthew 5:23-24.
    i) Unrepentant believers under discipline were excluded from the Supper (1 Cor. 5:11-13; 2 Thess. 3:6, 11-15).

4) Frequency
  a) 1st day of the week
    i) Act 20:7 And upon the first day of the week, when the disciples came together to break bread, Paul preached unto them, ready to depart on the morrow; and continued his speech until midnight.
    ii) The Didache and Justin Martyr's First Apology indicate that weekly administration of the Lord's Supper existed.

5) Process
  a) During the meeting the men, when led by the Spirit, can share a passage, prayer, or song in line with the purpose of this meeting 1 Corinthians 14:26. The time will conclude with the communion and then an intentional time to offer unto the Lord financially and other ways of giving of your life to the Lord.

Resources
- https://www.desiringgod.org/messages/what-is-baptism-and-how-important-is-it
- Understanding Baptism - Bobby Jamieson
- Ryrie, Charles Caldwell. Basic Theology: A Popular Systematic Guide to Understanding Biblical Truth (p. 488). Moody Publishers. Kindle Edition.
parsing_notes: Document is a teaching/position paper on the two church ordinances (Believers Baptism and the Lord's Supper). Structure: opening summary statements on both ordinances, then a deeper expansion on Baptism (sections 1-6) with a marker 'Covered till here' suggesting the document represents teaching delivered up to that point with the remainder used for the next session; then a John 3:5 excursus surveying three interpretations of 'water'; then a near-duplicate restatement of the opening summary on baptism; then an expanded section titled 'Ordinance 2 -- The Lord's Supper' covering institution, signification, requirements, frequency, and process; followed by a Resources list. The duplication between the opening summary and the later expansion is a feature of the source document; it has been preserved in raw_extracted_text but consolidated in the chunks (the second 'Believers Baptism' restatement is omitted as a duplicate). One verse reference in the source, 'Matt. 2:19' next to the Trinitarian formula, is almost certainly a typo for 'Matt. 28:19'; raw_extracted_text preserves the original, scripture_refs lists both as found, and chunk_02 corrects to 'Matt. 28:19' with a parenthetical 'cf.' for safety. Markdown 'mark' (highlight) and 'underline' annotations have been stripped from the chunk content but preserved meaning. No personal names or organisation names appear in the source aside from one external author attribution on a quoted summary (J. Piper), that attribution is a published-author citation (analogous to the Ryrie / Bobby Jamieson / Josh Moody / Justin Martyr / Didache resource citations) and is retained as a standard bibliographic reference, not as an internal-contributor attribution that the anonymisation rule targets. Two chunks contain perspectives_within_chunk: chunk_11 (three interpretations of 'water' in John 3:5) and chunk_14 (tension over whether the Lord's Table is restricted to baptised believers).
